data.title: VYM Vs. XYLD: Why The Dividend Growth ETF Beats The Popular High Yield Play
data.description:
Recently, covered call ETFs have become much more popular among income investors, especially those whose underlying indexes are either the S&P 500 (SPY) or the NASDAQ (QQQ). The reason for this is that they seem to give investors the best of all worlds, which includes strong underlying performance stemming from the high-performing mega-cap-tech-dominated indices, along with attractive monthly distributions that come from the covered call writing strategies employed by these funds.

A big source of justification for investors who choose these funds comes from both the higher yield and the higher total returns that they’ve delivered in recent years relative to many popular dividend growth ETFs. However, I think this analysis is short-sighted, and in today’s article, I am going to argue why one popular dividend growth ETF, the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM), makes for a better long-term dividend investment than a popular covered call ETF, the Global X S&P 500® Covered Call ETF (XYLD), does.

The Structural Difference Between VYM And XYLD

In particular, while XYLD is well diversified, given that it holds the S&P 500 as its underlying index, making it a potential one-stop shop for a passive income investor, I think that VYM is an even better diversified and overall superior option to XYLD. Additionally, you could always couple it with a couple of other higher-yielding diversifying funds like the Reeves Utility Income CEF (UTG), the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP), the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), and the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ), to boost the portfolio's aggregate yield while also further enhancing the portfolio's diversification, which would create a better overall risk-adjusted structure and more sustainable, attractive yield than XYLD does.

To better understand why VYM is a better core holding for an income investor than XYLD, we need to first look at how they are laid out. VYM tracks the FTSE High Dividend Yield Index, which screens for companies with above-average dividends. This translates into a portfolio with well over 500 holdings and oftentimes nearly 600 holdings (571 at present). In addition, it has broad sector exposure, with its largest exposure being to financials, technology, and healthcare, while industrials, consumer defensive stocks, and energy all have meaningful exposure in the portfolio as well.

It also manages to place pretty big bets on its top holdings, with its top 10 making up about 28% of its total portfolio. Particularly notable is the fact that VYM has a major AI player and a strong dividend growth stock in Broadcom (AVGO) as its top holding, making up a whopping 8.69% of its total portfolio.

Meanwhile, XYLD owns the underlying S&P 500 index, which means it actually has fewer holdings than VYM does, and it is also much more heavily exposed to mega-cap tech, which makes up over a third (~35%) of its total portfolio, then writes at-the-money index calls on the S&P 500 to generate very attractive monthly distributions, which have amounted to about a 12% trailing 12-month yield. It also makes its distributions monthly, which is an attractive feature for retirees. However, the at-the-money call writing strategy means that its upside is significantly capped, and this is seen from the fund’s long-term price performance, which has effectively been flat over the long term, even as the S&P 500 has rallied significantly.

Additionally, XYLD has a 10 times higher expense ratio than VYM does at 0.6% compared to 0.06% for VYM. Not only that, but XYLD has generated virtually nothing in terms of dividend growth over the long term, whereas VYM has grown its dividend for 19 straight years, and over the past 10 years, it has grown it at a 5% CAGR.

While the appeal of XYLD is clear, given its high current yield and exposure to the S&P 500, there are better ways to achieve these outcomes, including with a portfolio that has VYM at its core. This is because over the long term, VYM has outperformed XYLD on a total return basis over time. In fact, VYM's stock price performance alone (i.e., not counting its dividends) has outpaced XYLD's total returns over time, while XYLD's options writing strategy has failed to do much to smooth out its sequence of returns risk since those are very closely correlated with VYM's stock price performance, as evidenced in the chart below:

A Better Way To Build Sustainable Income

However, for investors who need a higher income yield than VYM offers in order to mitigate sequence of returns risk in retirement and live off of dividends alone, you can pair VYM with other higher-yielding funds. For example, combining UTG, AMLP, and RQI with VYM can generate an aggregate portfolio yield in the mid-single digits, while still generating strong dividend growth over time.

While VYM also still gives some decent tech exposure, for investors who want more mega-cap tech exposure, they can always add a Nasdaq-focused covered call ETF like GPIQ to the portfolio to bring in a high-yielding source of mega-cap tech exposure that’s more concentrated than what XYLD offers, since these funds are based on the NASDAQ 100 (NDX) underlying index instead of the S&P 500.

Investor Takeaway

While covered call funds are understandably popular right now, given their strong relative performance against dividend growth funds and their very attractive yields, there are much better ways to get exposure to that space than via XYLD. Additionally, for investors who want durable and sustainably growing dividend income along with capital appreciation, I would prefer to steer clear of covered call ETFs altogether, or at least keep them a very small part of the portfolio, and instead invest in quality dividend growth funds like VYM, and perhaps supplement them with either some high-yield funds like those mentioned in this article, or even better yet, build a portfolio of high-quality, undervalued, high-yielding stocks from the REITs (VNQ), MLPs, BDCs (BIZD), and broader infrastructure sectors.

This is what we do, and we have achieved considerable long-term total return outperformance in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: GDXY: Not Enough Upside, Too Aggressive On Income
data.description:
Back in April, when I was near the peak of "pounding the table" about gold and gold miners (even from before that, but I really started going around that time), I covered the YieldMax Gold Miners Option Income Strategy ETF (GDXY). I gave it a Hold rating, not because it was a bad time to consider gold or gold miners, but because I felt like owning the income variant of GDX, the global gold miners index that GDXY is based on, was a better bet in the short-term. I saw that it had the potential for explosive upside, and I wouldn't want to limit that.

Maybe I was a little harsh in saying that YieldMax was "striking out" on gold, but the expectations I had played out. Even assuming all dividends reinvested (which is the best way I can show returns, but is unreasonable for income investors), GDXY trailed GDX by a significant margin. This chart starts at the publication date of my first article on GDXY, April 22nd.

But Income is Income

I understand that income investors are often not phased by the chart above, and some even get angry with me (vocal minority, I assume) when I bring it up, see the comments sections of most YieldMax ETF articles.

On the first part, I understand the idea: if you need income, and you're interested in earning it without selling shares, trading your upside for immediate, high income could be a reasonable trade-off. When it comes to most stocks, or even gold itself, I get it.

My problem with GDXY isn't about income vehicles. I understand that trading upside for high immediate income is the deal that income investors want when they buy these funds. They're just willing to accept that top chart and so going on and on about it doesn't make a lot of sense -- they don't care, that's already assumed going into the trade.

My problem with GDXY is similar across almost all YieldMax funds, and that is that I believe they are too aggressive with their income generation. They get so aggressive that their funds tend to kick out insanely high distribution rates, which can be alluring for investors, but it leads to dramatic underperformance in the long term because reinvestment is necessary to stabilize the funds long-term.

GDX's distribution rate is currently sitting at 58.2%. And it leads to a chart like this, showing the difference in return of price and total return. If we assume that income investors keep all the income that is spit out from the fund instead of reinvesting, investors who bought at GDXY's inception are now down nearly 15%. That means that moving forward, assuming distribution rates remain the same, their investment will earn a lower nominal dollar amount next year.

And this will continue, compounding downward. Keep in mind that this negative return happened in one of the most explosive bull markets for the underlying asset in the last decade. In a gold miner bear market, I would expect GDXY to drop even further, and without reinvestment from income investors, their nominal income stream will continue to shrink over time in.

It may stay by percentage terms, but income investors typically don't spend money in percentage terms, they spend money in nominal terms.

We can see this play out in the nominal dividends paid out by the fund. Keep in mind that YieldMax switched from monthly to weekly paying, and so the drop in amount earlier this year is due to the distribution frequency changes. The "D" symbol below looks cluttered, but I needed to include it to show when the dividends were actually paid and to show why the large drop in dividends paid. If we average the weeklies back out to a month, then the previous trend is clearer.

If we total up November's dividend amounts, it comes out to $0.58, on the low end of the trend, but not hurting so badly yet. But again, this is a problem that will come up after owning this for years, not something that will come up after a couple of months.

GDXY Overview

Now that I've said my piece on YieldMax (yes, I understand the irony of saying that a company with that name is too aggressive), let's dig into the fund itself.

GDXY fundamentally is a bet on the global gold miners index (which the VanEck GDX ETF captures), with the upside capped in favor of immediate, high income. This is the trade that covered calls inherently come with, and one that income investors expect when employing this fund.

GDXY itself was brought to market in May 2024, carries a gross expense ratio of 1.08%, has net assets in excess of $222M, and pays its dividends weekly.

Like most of YieldMax's strategies, this one also relies on synthetic options, meaning that call and put options are married together to create the same exposure profile as holding long stock, but without the transaction costs of holding long stock. This allows them to hold nearly the entire portfolio in treasuries while still gaining the exposure to gold. In the holdings, we can even see longer-dated holdings, such as an obligation due in 2031.

Once a synthetic position is created, credit spreads, which are more capital efficient for YieldMax to employ, are sold short to limit the upside and generate the high income.

Looking at the options held by GDXY, I was able to produce this chart, which shows the current profit and loss profile of GDXY. Note that after publication of this article, this will likely be incorrect, as YieldMax is able to trade intraday and often changes their holdings day-to-day to accommodate for the fast-moving nature of the options market. I would expect the overall structure of the chart to remain the same.

This cap on the upside, although not entirely flat and slightly elevated, does create a situation where investors are not meeting their full upside potential and instead prioritizing income. That priority creates a 58% distribution rate, but it means that investors will be lagging behind dramatically. More than I believe most investors would like to lag behind.

Of course, the trade-off is up to the individual. If you're an income investor who does need 58% distribution rates, which GDXY has provided so far, and that's notable.

The Gold Miner Thesis

To be bullish on GDXY, one needs to necessarily be bullish on its underlying asset, GDX, the global gold miner's index. These firms can be very volatile (a primary reason they were selected by YieldMax to be a covered call target), much more than gold itself, and that is what many investors want when investing in them. There is extra upside (and downside) for gold miners over gold itself as an asset. Here is the YTD of both assets for reference:

There are good reasons to be bullish on gold itself right now, which would translate to gold miner bullishness. From a recent article I wrote earlier this month, "Gold Has a New Buyer, A Different Kind of Central Bank," I discussed how institutional buying is still making up much of the gold demand, and that new kinds of institutions were buying gold, like stablecoin issuer Tether.

I see the structural demand for gold as growing, and this includes not only central banks but now also another kind of central bank: cryptocurrency issuers. Gold is being bought up as part of a means of backing USDT, which makes up 60% of the stablecoin market. This is a huge shift, and one that shouldn't be ignored.

Of course, speculation is still rampant, and gold's recent volatility has proven that while U.S. investors are not accumulating and allocating to gold like those in the rest of the world, they are still trading it heavily. I expect that volatility to continue moving forward, but that is not necessarily a bad thing. It does warrant caution.

This translates to demand for gold miners who provide the supply to meet it, and who disproportionately benefit during run-ups in gold itself, as they can lock in high prices with futures contracts and make significant returns. The index itself is made up of giants in the space, such as Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) and Newmont Corp (NEM), who have excelled in the space during this latest leg of the gold bull market.

Of course, if gold were to turn, gold miners would likely turn with it and likely be more volatile, so this is a risk to consider.

A structural turn would require central banks to slow or halt their purchases significantly, breaking current trends across the globe. This may happen suddenly, especially if central banks that are working to de-dollarize their holdings are unwilling to finance more gold purchases at current levels. I do not seriously consider this a threat right now, because current demand seems to be fairly linear, but if central banks do halt their buying, it will likely come all at once (just like their demand seemed to do).

Much of gold's momentum is based on these institutional trends continuing to play out, and there is an "ETF trapdoor" so to speak because many of the publicly available tools investors can use to speculate on the price of gold being are backed by paper gold and not physical gold. This means that a sell off in these assets, also sparked by speculation, would not cause much physical gold to actually change hands, but could affect the price dramatically.

That would affect the valuations of gold miners, as they would be able to fetch lower prices for the gold they produce, and I would expect GDX and subsequently GDXY to have an even worse response.

An Alternative Fund With More Upside

I am not a huge fan of Roundhill ETFs, generally speaking, for many of the same reasons as I'm not a huge fan of YieldMax ETFs. I believe that they tend to be overly aggressive with their options positioning, which creates situations with unsustainable levels of distribution. Over many years, the erosion of NAV without reinvestment and the erosion of principal without reinvestment will be problematic for future income streams. While the percentages may remain the same, the nominal income would be expected to drop over time as you have less and less principal to generate income with.

But when I pit GDXY against Roundhill's alternative, GDXW, which has a far shorter lifespan and so the comparison is limited, I see a fund that has significantly more upside to it. Here we can see both funds capturing the explosive growth of gold miners that happened in this last month, with GDXW coming out 10% ahead in total return.

From a price perspective, it's worse. But it also highlights how low the payments are for GDXW than they are for GDXY. An investor who is only focused on the income may actually prefer the lower performance for the higher income. I believe that that's folly in the long term, but I understand where the impulse would come from and why it may appear optimal now if current income is more valuable than future income to you.

GDXY and GDXW are comparable because they have incredibly similar strategies, both focused on selling options against GDX for income. GDXW has a lower expense ratio at 0.99%, although I believe the difference between the two is splitting hairs at the end of the day as they are so close, but fee-sensitive investors should take note that GDXY's expense ratio is 1.08%.

The major difference between the two, and what explains the differences in return, is that GDXW uses swaps to generate their returns, and seeks for 1.2x leverage against GDX while it caps the upside, meaning that it should have more upside capture in general, but does come with some extra risk.

To this end, GDXW has one holding:

In a bullish market, this translates to excess gains, as we can see. However, it could also work against the fund in a bearish scenario. Since I am bullish on gold miners, this seems like a good trade-off to me, but make sure that your risk tolerance would allow for this if you decide to look into GDXW as an alternative to GDXY.

A Note on Taxes

One thing YieldMax does have going for it, although this also applies to Roundhill and all other ETFs that use options to generate income, is that they are very tax-efficient.

When distributed through an ETF structure, options gains and losses are treated as return of capital ("ROC"). This is considered advantageous because ROC does not generate taxable events. Instead, it counts against your cost basis, and so taxes are deferred so long as you continue to hold the shares. If you hold a fund long enough, your cost basis can go to zero. Any distributions after that will create taxable events but be counted as long-term capital gains in the year that you receive the distributions.

This makes GDXY not only more efficient than trading the options yourself, but also makes it viable for taxable accounts because it could reduce tax liabilities given that your long-term capital gains rate is lower than your income tax rate, which is usually true for most people.

In the latest 19A-1 form released by YieldMax ETFs, GDXY's distribution was estimated at 95% return of capital. Last year's final ROC amount, found on this IRS form published on the YieldMax ETFs website, was at 68%.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I am keeping my Hold rating on GDXY, not because I am less bullish on gold miners at all. In fact, you can read my recent buy for a gold miner-linked ETF, GDMN, here.

No, my Hold rating stays because I do not believe that the YieldMax strategy is the best option for income investors to consider. It is not a bad option, and for that reason, I'm not giving it a Sell rating. But it is definitely not the most optimal strategy given that I believe it over-distributes and is overly aggressive in its option trading, which will lead to a necessarily crumbling net asset value over time. From my view, that strategy will not lead to long-term success in the fund without necessary reinvestment.

Because I don't like to advise income investors to reinvest portions of their income, although that would make the strategy more attractive (and could make GDXY a Buy rating given my bullish outlook on gold miners), I must keep my Hold rating on GDXY. For now, investors should consider other options like uncapped exposure to gold and gold miners, as well as GDXW, which seems to distribute less and capture more upside in the index -- something I find preferable in our current environment.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: V.F. Corp.: Continued Drag From Lackluster Brand Power
data.description:
Summary

V.F. Corporation (VFC) is a retail company with a history of mergers and acquisitions. In an effort to improve key brands like Timberland, The North Face, and Vans, management is currently executing a “reinvent” turnaround to cut costs, grow revenues, and lower its debt. But the prevailing retail picture is not great, with tariffs and lackluster brand power creating headwinds for the business over the next six to twelve months. Because the company has relied on acquisitions, a key question remains as to whether or not management can execute a turnaround organically. With previous goodwill impairments as an indicator, V.F. Corporation may find it difficult to grab share of consumer preferences, making for a decent short opportunity in a difficult macroenvironment.

Background: Annual Report, Second Quarter Summary

V.F. Corporation is a retail trading company that owns multiple brands, including Vans, The North Face, and Timberland, among others. Management recently sold off the Dickies brand, leaving VFC with ten total brands. The company has a long history of mergers & divestitures; notably, they acquired Vans in 2004 for $396 million, The North Face for a mere $25 million in 2000, and Timberland for $2 billion in 2011. But the history of M&A has led to lackluster stock performance, with VFC share prices dropping over 75% in the past five years. Now, at a time when the company is facing revenue declines and brand impairments, management must fix the balance sheet by reducing debt, streamlining cost cutting, and “reinventing” itself in order to get back on track. As of the most recent Annual Report, CEO Bracken Darrell highlighted reduced net debt by 26%, cost savings of $300 million, and a Vans brand turnaround, and as of Q2 fiscal 2026, the sale of Dickies for $600 million in order to pay down debt.

While each of the aforementioned initiatives has been positive for V.F. Corp., the question remains as to whether or not management can brand-rebuild (specifically Vans) when most of its history has been M&A focused. In the meantime, through fiscal Q2, revenues have been roughly flat year over year, interest expenses have crept up from $90 to $93 million, and inventory has drifted upward over the past three months. If I were to be reflective on the second quarter (three months ending September 30th), I would say that both COGS and SG&A expenses were down, leading to a solid $75 million increase in operating income. Furthermore, the inventory build may just be seasonality as management prepares for the holiday season. On the second-quarter conference call, the CEO highlighted 4% revenue growth for The North Face, 4% growth for Timberland, and great results for Altra (forecasting $250 million in yearly revenue). But with Vans revenue down 11%, the brand will continue to be a drag on earnings, and I would not be surprised to see impairments down the road. On top of that, VFC is navigating a difficult macroeconomic environment for retail, as it relates to tariffs, a Q3 forecast for revenues down 1-3%, and the waning strength of the consumer.

Tariffs and the Consumer

Even though V.F. Corp. spreads out its sourcing, with 273 independent contractors in 30 countries, they are still exposed to tariff risk at the hands of the current White House Administration. Per the Annual Report, 85% of the products are sourced from Southeast Asia, Central, and South America (p. 26). The report notes Vietnam, Bangladesh, Cambodia, and Indonesia are the top four countries for sourcing. As an aside, I will lean briefly on Nike’s (NKE) Q2 results, as the company’s margins and inventory were affected by tariffs; notably, Nike’s gross margin decreased three percent, and inventory costs were higher. Specific to the Nike conference call, the CEO stated that tariffs are presenting a headwind to any margin expansion. Anecdotally, the company manufactures 51%, 28%, and 17% of its footwear in Vietnam, Indonesia, and China (respectively), as well as 31%, 15%, and 15% of its apparel in Vietnam, China, and Cambodia (respectively; see Nike’s Annual Report, p. 3). The recent performance of Nike as a retail bellwether points toward additional tariff risk for V.F. Corp.; the “reinvent” strategy employed by management may not be enough to stave off industry headwinds. Even though the CEO is projecting $500-$600 million in operating income expansion, that target may come well beyond 2028.

The overarching state of the consumer is one of higher housing costs and lower discretionary spending. According to the National Retail Federation, November 2025 core retail sales showed 4.66% growth year over year but were flat month over month; specifically, clothing and accessories ticked down 0.04% month over month. It is perhaps the calm before the holiday swell, but recent data by the NRF regarding holiday trends indicate the consumer is holding back a bit on holiday spending.

In the slide above, we can see that consumer spending is expected to drop about 2% ($13 per person) from $641 to $628, with roughly half of that expected to go toward clothing and accessories:

Given the above data, there is an important dichotomy between 1) V.F. Corp. management projecting revenues down 1-3% in their most important quarter of the year and 2) Consumer retail growing 4.66% year over year (while holiday spending will tick down) that speaks to a long-term issue with V.F. Corp.’s branding and its ability to turn around the business organically. In my opinion, over the next twelve months tariff issues will persist, and the consumer will remain pressured by inflation & costs, making VFC shares a sell.

Goodwill Impairments and Valuation

We’ve mentioned previously that V.F. Corp. has been an amalgamation of acquisitions since its founding. That said, it is worth looking at some of these deals in order to gauge valuation metrics. The company acquired Dickies in 2017 for $820 million, and Vans was acquired in 2004—both for approximately 1x sales. The $2 billion Timberland acquisition in 2011 added about $700 million in sales at the time, making the EV/sales multiple roughly 2.8x. The one standout has been The North Face, which was near bankruptcy until VF management scooped up the business for a total enterprise value of $155 million; that year, The North Face reportedly generated $238 million in sales (a 0.65x EV/sales multiple). If we read the tea leaves, anything between 0.65x and, say, 2.8x EV/Sales is a decent multiple, assuming a good macroeconomic climate.

Per Seeking Alpha, at $18.50/share, VFC trades around 1.3x forward EV/Sales. Perhaps it is trading at fair value; however, the business is operating in a difficult retail environment with flailing brand power (re: Vans and the “reinvent” turnaround). As such, in my opinion a 1x multiple is appropriate, leading toward a price of $15/share or lower. With Vans revenues down 16% annually, and downtrodden revenue guidance for the holiday quarter, I believe the downside is much more. Note that with the Dickies sale alone, V.F. Corp. lost roughly $120 million on the deal.

Something to watch out for in the coming quarters includes goodwill impairments, if for no other reason than that is what has happened in the past. In 2024, management identified $507.6 million in impairments for the Dickies, Icebreaker, and Timberland brands. One year later, there were additional impairments of $89.2 million for Dickies (p. 28 of the Annual Report). While there have been no impairment charges for the first two quarters of fiscal 2026, note that the past impairments have occurred in the third & fourth fiscal quarters—right where we are at now. Additionally, I will be keeping a close eye on balance sheet inventory over the next six months. If holiday sales are less than expected, inventories could remain high, forcing management into higher discounting next year.

Risks

While I am anticipating a rough six months for the business, I could be wrong on several fronts. First off, we know that retail spending has increased year over year across the board: if VFC manages to capture more of that spend, then the company could execute its turnaround and increase gross profit by the expected $500-$600 million goal set by management. The crux of my thesis largely depends upon the state of the consumer as well as the Vans brand's drag on sales. On the latest conference call, the CEO mentioned product initiatives for Vans. Consider his commentary below in response to a Vans question on revenue growth:

“So our expectation is as we keep rolling in newer and newer product into the stores, we’re going to see more and more performance, and yes, we’re obviously upgrading our marketing. […] You’ll see a shift away from the skate-only marketing into really that plus a lot more. You’ll see surfers, you’ll see a lot more product. […] So we’re just going to keep pouring it on. This is a fundamentals business. You got to be in the right places with the right products with the right story. And we think we’re really going to have that as we go forward.”

If the Vans brand ends up catching on with consumers, it would be a great success for management (and bad for a short position). Time will tell, but for now I lean on the side of declining brand power.

Similarly, the tariff risk across retail rests on the whims of the Trump Administration as well as the Supreme Court. Recently, the Treasury Secretary mentioned his expectation that a Supreme Court ruling on Learning Resources Inc. v. Trump will come down in January. If the Court sides with retailers, the White House would be forced to remove and/or refund tariffs; the ancillary benefit would go directly to a company like V.F. Corp.

Conclusion

The retail landscape for V.F. Corp. remains cloudy, and I expect the company to post lackluster earnings over the next six to twelve months. We will know much more come January & February, when we find out the results of holiday sales as well as a potential ruling on tariffs. But for the time being, VFC, with its history of mixed merger & acquisition outcomes, stands to lose roughly 15% or more of its value, with the possibility of goodwill impairments to come down the road. Vans will continue to be a drag on earnings, making the “reinvent” turnaround harder to execute. All told, VFC is a short opportunity over the next six to twelve months, as the company works to navigate multiple headwinds coming toward it.

data.title: Why Ichor's 2026 Recovery Is Underestimated
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Multiple catalysts combined, provide evidence that Ichor Holding (ICHR) fundamentals will improve in 2026 and the share price is in the process of bottoming. I am inclined to assign a BUY rating because of the following.

Firstly, last month former CTO, Philip Barros was named CEO, signaling a shift towards operational discipline and innovation. He is well-suited for the role and capable of making the right calls on supply chain issues or more focused M&A. Mr. Barros labeled 2026 as a transition year for Ichor focusing on profitability.

Secondly, two main clients, Lam Research and Applied Materials are expected to grow their revenues respectively +12.5% and +8.7% CAGR for 2026-2029 period. Therefore, I expect Ichor holdings to ramp-up its production and improve gross margins towards their historical 20% levels from current 10-12% levels. Moreover, despite only being in a test phase, proprietary flow control and valve products destined to various industrial applications, optimizing energy efficiency are expected to drive margins upwards.

Finally, this is a company with a prudent 1.2x net leverage. The $125 million facility refinancing exercise in September 2025, the $100 million RCF and an additional $175 million incremental facility available provides the management the ability to peruse its M&A strategy.

Ichor Holdings underperformed the broader market and is down -45.3% YTD because of the cyclical nature of the semiconductors downstream industry, highly dependent on main OEMs production schedules, creating an interesting entry point at $17.6.

The company trades at 0.9x book value, 18% discount to the median of selected peers and has 56% margin of safety.

What does the Company Actually do?

Ichor Holding is a niche semiconductor player supplying fluid delivery subsystems for large OEMs such as Applied Digital Corporation (APLD), Lam Research Corporation (LRCX) or ASML Holdings N.V. (ASML). If these two were the “Mainframes” of the Wafer Fabrication, Ichor would play more of a “plumbing” role. It specializes in gas and chemical deliveries subsystems, flow control products and precision components. These tools are used in the etching process, removing material from the silicon wafer to create patterns that will form electronic circuits designed by the lithography.

Ichor holdings competes mainly with Ultra Clean Holdings, Inc. (UCTT), MKS Inc. (MKSI) and Entegris, Inc. (ENTG). Each of them has its own singularities of course, that I detail in the table below however, all of them are suppliers to Tier 1 OEMs such as Lam Research and Applied Materials or ASML Holding. Ultra Clean Holdings, stands out as the most direct competitor for gas delivery systems products, even if it also has a large “Service” division for cleaning and coating used parts.

Ichor has deep relationship with the mentioned OEMs and has the required regional scale manufacturing in the U.S., Singapore, Malaysia, U.K., Korea and Mexico. As of 2024, Lam Research and Applied Materials represented together 73% of Ichor Holdings’ revenue. In order to drive margins upwards, Ichor is currently developing its proprietary flow controls and valve products that will create differentiation compared to Ultra Clean Holdings.

As of today, Ichor Holdings has a $605 million in Market Cap, $676 million Enterprise Value, which implies a 0.6x forward EV/ Rev, 8.8x EBITDA and 23.5x PE multiples. More detailed information of the Equity to Enterprise Value bridge is presented below.

The Share Price Evolution

Ichor has significantly underperformed the Semiconductor VanEck ETF (SMH) as the market favored IP-rich equipment suppliers with higher margins. 60-months beta stands at 1.82 with an historical volatility of 53% making Ichor a highly cyclical play where small changes in margins or guidance can have an outsized impact on the share price. The market tends to usually overshoot in one or another direction leading to a large sell-off as it is the case here or sharp rallies and de facto create opportunities for entries.

Ichor share price crashed -45.3% YTD vs. -28.8% for Ultra Clean Holdings. Both companies were impacted by declining earnings in recent quarters and soft guidance. What is striking is that both names have lagged dramatically compared to their main customers: Lam Research shares are up +138.5% YTD while Applied Materials are up +57.7%. For sure, Ichor and Ultra Clean sit downstream and are late in the semi cycle, but I would expect volume to ramp-up much faster than this, usually in one to three quarters. It seems that here the markets expect several quarters of improvement before giving any credit to these two cyclical names. More details on the peers share price performance are available on the chart below.

Most Recent Quarterly Performance

Ichor quarterly revenue growth has been erratic reflecting uneven OEMs build schedules and Ichor’s growth slipped into negative territory for the past two quarters. It is clear that the large customer concentration did not play in the company’s favor. Net income stayed negative for the past 5 quarters but operating cash flow margin is holding up around 8-12% margin.

During the conference call, the management guided for a 4Q25 revenues range of $210-230 million with 10-12% gross margin. I except 4Q25 revenues to be around $220 million which would represent an 8.1% sequential quarter decline, and therefore a FY 2025 revenue of $944.1 million, which stands at +11.2% YoY compared to +4.7% in FY 2024. Sequential quarterly performance isn’t stellar, but we can notice YoY stabilization already.

Financial Projections

As far as the projections are concerned, I see growth rates for 2026 and 2027 to accelerate above than the current analyst consensus of +5.4% and +9.8%, that I find rather pessimistic.

In order to understand better potential Ichor growth trajectory, I look at the financial projections of its two main customers: Lam Research and Applied Materials. Both have different fiscal year ends, but the table below gives us perspective on their revenue growth, cost of goods sold evolution, where ultimately Ichor is the direct beneficiary and finally their capex plan through 2025-2029. Lam Research and Applied Materials are expected to grow their revenues +12.5% and +8.8% on average for the 2026-2029 period as presented in the table below.

While Lam Research, being smaller and higher beta than Applied Materials, it shows continuous and aggressive revenue growth. COGS are growing accordingly as the company scales, which is a net positive for Ichor Holdings. Interestingly enough, Lam Research seems to have a better operating leverage, generating more profits for every dollar of sales compared to Applied Materials. It is worth noting that the sluggish 2026 growth is related to the fact that Applied Materials has a heavy exposure to China and export control are expected to reduce its 4Q25 revenues by $110 million and 1Q25 by $600 million.

CapEx for Lam Research is expected to slow down in 2026, then continue to grow nicely afterwards as there is a continuous need to invest in Gate-All-Around transistors or advanced packaging for etch/deposition tools. Applied Materials CapEx is already 2-3x Lam Research’s and it is projected to decrease over the years. I expect Ichor holding to be direct beneficially of such performance.

As an additional point of reference, Reuters expects semiconductor equipment sales for 2026 to growth +9% in 2026 and +7.3% in 2027 to $135 billion. Therefore, I estimate Ichor Holdings to grow +12.0% in 2026 and +8.0% in 2027. I assume margins to improve gradually, from 7.3% EBITDA margin in 2026 to 10.1% in 2029. Additional details on the projections can be found in the table below.

Valuation Overview

Looking at the trading multiples of the peers shown in the table below, we see direct correlation between the EBITDA margins and the P/E multiples. Usually in the semiconductor sector, higher EBITDA margins are related to an IP ownership or a strong pricing power that directly warrants a P/E multiple premia, especially if a company can generate a ROE above 20%. Currently Ichor trades at forward 2025 P/E of 23.5x which represents a discount of 22.6% compared to the peer’s median forward multiple of 30.3x. Excluding Lam Research and Applied Materials the discount stands at 18.2%. It is crystal clear from the table below that Ichor Holding trades roughly in line with Ultra Clean Holdings (23.5x vs. 24.3x). Overall, as long as the margins for Ichor are compressed, the P/E expansion seems to be limited, but we know that this will be exactly the focus of the newly appointed CEO.

For the DCF analysis, we factor in the projections previously discussed in junction with a hypothesis of a 10.73% WACC, a standard level in Semiconductor Equipment according to Damodaran and a terminal value growth rate of +3.0%. Important to note that CapEx levels are assumed to remain around 3-4% of total revenues across the 2026-2034 period. On balance, the DCF analysis yields an intrinsic value for Ichor Holdings of $27.6, which represents a +54% margin of safety from the current price of $17.6. Additional details can be found in the table below.

It is no surprise that the DCF results are highly sensitive to the terminal growth rate. Everything else being equal, a +4% TGR input would yield a 71% margin safety. Details on WACC and TGR sensitivities are presented below. These are useful for “a stress test case”, and here we can see that even with only +1% TGR, Ichor holding would still have +39% margin of safety as long as it performs according to the forecasts.

Important Risks Involved

Overall, pressure on margins remains a challenge. For 2023 and 2024, Ichor Holdings posted negative EBIT and earnings, as revenue came softer and R&D expenses continued to progress. However, cash from operations remained positive at $57.6 million in 2023 and $27.9 million in 2024. Moreover, in 2024, Ichor repaid $120.6 million of debt after having issued $144.5 million of stock, effectively helping the cash flow going forward and the cash balance which, as of 2024 stood at stood at $108.7 million. As such, its core business is not burning through cash. As per the latest report as of September 2025, Ichor cash and cash equivalent position amounts to $92.5 million, giving the company flexibility in case of a cyclical downturn. Additionally, the company has access to $100 million RCF as mentioned above.

High customer concentration comes with inherent risks for Ichor Holding stock. However, this would be only an issue if Applied Materials, Lam Research or ASML decide to manufacture their own gas delivery subsystems. I think, these products are critical but not differentiating enough for these giants and it would duplicate fixed costs for them.

Commoditization of the Ichor products drives pricing downwards and adds pressure to the margins, however the company is looking to penetrate secondary markets such as aerospace and defense and medical. This was actually the purpose when acquiring IMG in November 2021 that brings deep expertise in high-precision metal fabrication and joining technologies.

Investment Decision

I am inclined to believe that the change in CEO focusing on improving margins by sourcing internally the products and limiting the non-semiconductor business will yield its results. Ichor’s main customers are expected to do very well in the future and there is always some lag downstream where Ichor sits. Looks like 2025 numbers are about to stabilize and all the bad news have already been priced in. In my opinion, the risk reward here is favorable for the next 12 months. Blended valuation from trading peers’ multiples and the DCF analysis imply a +37% upside from the current levels.

data.title: Zimmer Biomet: Execution Remains The Sticking Point
data.description:
My mentor on Wall Street often liked to use the analogy of a duck swimming across a pond when talking about certain companies – while everything on the surface may look serene, there’s a lot of active paddling going on beneath. That image comes to mind for me with respect to Zimmer Biomet (ZBH) (“Zimmer”), as there has been a lot of effort put into improving the business, but the actual visible results have yet to be all that dynamic or impressive.

Zimmer shares are down another 16% since my last update on the company, underperforming the broader medical device space by around 20% and likewise underperforming Smith & Nephew (SNN) and Stryker (SYK), while Enovis (ENOV) managed to do even worse.

Zimmer shares look undervalued, but a bull thesis requires some measure of faith that management is going to turn the corner on execution and rebuild Street confidence in the story. There are certainly some valid drivers here, but given that improved execution remains one of the key “to-do’s” to close the gap with Stryker, and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) to some extent, there is a value trap risk here.

Frustration Remains A Common Issue

Execution, and consistency of execution, has been a key talking point around Zimmer for some time, as management has been saying the right things for years about how to make the business more competitive and value-generative but has more often than not come up a little short with the actual results.

The last quarter was a good example – management was pretty vocal about a second-half ramp and “scratching 6%” growth in the third quarter, but reported organic revenue growth came in at 5% and a bit below Street expectations. While management blamed distributor issues in the Mideast, Eastern Europe, and Latin America, the reality is that the company continues to lose share across its hip and knee franchises despite its “Magnificent Seven” roster of new growth-driving products.

With those challenges, management reduced its organic growth guidance to 3.5% to 4% for FY’25, down from the 3% to 5% guide to start the year. I’m guardedly positive on the upcoming fourth quarter report given a little bit of momentum in the U.S. knee business, but I can’t say I feel especially confident about that call, and hitting its marks on margins will be important for sentiment.

Management Hopes New Products Will Reverse Ongoing Share Losses

Depending upon how the fourth quarter goes, there’s a chance that 2025 will mark the fifth straight year of global hip implant share loss, and it’s more likely that the company will log its fifth straight year of share loss in the global knee implant market. With growth in the industry slowing to the mid-single-digits, it’s tough for Zimmer to report enough growth to satisfy the Street while still ceding share to Stryker and JNJ.

There are some positive drivers in place that should help stop the losses and maybe reinvigorate some growth. More and more joint replacement procedures are being performed in ambulatory surgery centers (or ASCs), and this is now a key channel for the industry.

It’s hard to find accurate current data, but it seems likely that ASCs now account for more than 25% of all hip and knee surgeries. So while Zimmer has done a good job of accelerating its exposure to this channel since the pandemic (a pre-pandemic penetration rate in the low single digits to around 20% now), it still has work to do to keep up with this important channel.

Beyond sales channel efforts, the company has also been refreshing its product portfolio, with its share of sales from new products climbing from the low single digits to also around 20% now. The company’s Persona Osseo Ti knee now makes up nearly half of its U.S. knee business, and the Persona Revision and Oxford partial knee are important recent additions that should help reclaim some share, particularly the Oxford, given that it offers some product differentiation in the cementless partial knee space (a roughly $1B market) like mobile bearings and better anatomic fit. Elsewhere, there are offerings like Z1 and Hammr in the hip space that should help drive some business improvement.

Zimmer has also been stepping up its game in automation and navigation. While Zimmer’s ROSA robotic system hasn’t had the same success in driving share gains for the company as Stryker’s MAKO, the company’s acquisition of Monogram could ultimately drive more useful robotic systems that offer real advantages over MAKO and could start to drive share gains in three or four years (the timeframe management has laid out for a fully autonomous system using Zimmer implants).

Beyond this, the company has also acquired its way into a stronger foot/ankle franchise with the Paragon 28 deal – buying a credible player (roughly 5% share in a market where the leaders don’t have much more than 10% share) outgrowing the market by 2x-3x. There is also an arguably underrated soft tissue repair franchise here, with Tapestry potentially capable of taking share in rotator cuff and other soft tissue repair markets from the likes of Smith & Nephew and Arthrex.

The Outlook

I do like Zimmer’s recent M&A deals; I see value for Zimmer in both an expanded/improved foot/ankle business portfolio and more technological capabilities in robotics, navigation, and procedure planning. While the debt level here is higher than I’d like, I do think the company could still continue to make some smaller deals to further build up its IT/IP estate and/or add select product/procedure exposure in soft tissue and extremities.

I’m looking for close to 5% revenue growth over the next three years, a little more than 4% growth over the next five years, and long-term growth in the high 3%’s. I do expect to see some share improvement in major joints, offset by further slowing in those markets, and there is upside here if new implants, robotics/navigation, and/or soft tissue products grab/drive more share than I expect.

On the margin side, I don’t have a lot of confidence in Zimmer breaking much beyond the mid-30%s for adjusted EBITDA margin, but something like two to three points of long-term margin improvement would still be material in terms of driving improved free cash flow generation. With that, and some modest improvements in capital efficiency, I expect FCF margins to improve into the 17%-18% range, driving around 6% long-term annualized FCF growth.

Those cash flows can support a fair value around $100/share today, and again, there are opportunities to exceed these expectations. Likewise, growth/margin-driven EV/EBITDA is not demanding now, as a 3.5x forward revenue multiple is on the lower range of what seems “fair” (and thus does factor in a discount for Wall Street’s distrust of management) and still supports a fair value close to $116.

The Bottom Line

A few beat-and-raise quarters could do wonders to rebuild Street trust in Zimmer, and improved sales execution would be even more impactful for the long term if it leads to actual share gains in its core implant businesses. This is all possible, but it’s hard to call it probable given the company’s history, including recent trends over the last couple of years. The value is there if Zimmer can execute, but I can’t get more positive on the shares until I have more evidence in hand that they can in fact do that.

data.title: Broadcom: AI Beneficiary, Growing Backlog, Cheap Valuations, And Oversold Indicators
data.description:
I previously covered Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO, NEOE:AVGO:CA) in October 2025, discussing how OpenAI had sustained the ongoing AI mania to new heights, with the former likely to experience a drastic increase in its AI Serviceable Addressable Market [SAM].

Combined with the growing custom AI market share with existing customers, the upsides arising from the likely to be raised consensus forward estimates, the promising technical indicators, and the growing multi-year RPOs, I had upgraded AVGO to a Buy then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the AVGO stock, with the recent sideways trading cadence already triggering an excellent margin of safety as observed in the cheaper valuations, aided by the raised consensus forward estimates.

These developments build upon their excellent ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom across the infrastructure layer through the growing custom AI accelerator orders/backlog and across the SaaS layers through the improved pricing power/operating leverage.

AVGO Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status Across Infrastructure/SaaS Layers

AVGO 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, many AI-related stocks have entered a much needed correction/consolidation stage after a period of extreme exuberance in October 2025 and the market wakes up to valuation risks and elevated debt risks by November 2025.

Despite so, readers must note that AVGO remains well supported at the established Q4'25 floor of $320s - with it underscoring the emergence of bullish support for the king of custom AI accelerator segment during the multi-year cloud super cycle.

The same has been reiterated by the management in the recent FQ4'25 earnings call, with the growing consolidated backlog of $162B (+47.2% QoQ) and the guidance of "$73 billion in AI backlog to be delivered over the next 18 months" offering interested investors with an excellent insight into its intermediate term top/bottom-lines.

These reasons may also be why AVGO has felt confident to offer a promising FQ1'26 overall revenue guidance of $19.1B (+35.9% QoQ/+28.1% YoY) and adj EBITDA margin guidance of 67% (+2 points QoQ/-1 YoY), with it building upon the FY2025 outperformance at +23.8% YoY and +34.8% YoY, respectively.

Much of the tailwinds are attributed to the growing AI semiconductor opportunities at FQ1'26 revenues guidance of $8.2B (+26.1% QoQ/+100% YoY), as the segment notably drives the company's renewed growth prospects across custom AI accelerators and ethernet AI switches.

Particularly, AVGO has revealed the identity of the customer whom previously ordered "$10B of orders of AI racks" in the last earnings call, namely Anthropic, with the customer notably placing another $11B order "for delivery in late 2026."

This is on top of the new "fifth XPU customer through a $1 billion order placed for delivery in late 2026," with it underscoring the growing success of its custom AI accelerator offerings, as some of its customers, Alphabet (GOOG) also monetize the TPU offerings through external customers, including Apple (AAPL), Cohere, and Safe Superintelligence [SSI].

With certain analysts already highlighting GOOG TPU's lower Total Cost of Ownership [TCO] at $78.5M against Nvidia (NVDA) H100 at $177M, based on the assumption of "1,000-chip cluster running 24/7 inference workloads at 80% utilization," it is unsurprising that rumors already surface surrounding the potential adoption of TPUs by Meta (META).

This is on top of the supposed partnership between GOOG-META to develop the "TorchTPU" initiative to "significantly reduce switching costs for companies that want alternatives to Nvidia’s GPUs" without having to "undertaking significant, extra engineering work."

This development makes sense indeed, since many AI developers supposedly already use PyTorch, "an open-source project... which is a collection of pre-written code libraries and frameworks that automate many common tasks in developing AI software" - all of which triggering long-term growth tailwinds for AVGO as GOOG's strategic TPU partner.

These reasons may also be why AVGO has hinted "for that $73 billion (of booking) over the 18 months to keep growing," thanks to their ability to absorb more orders given:

  • their current lead time of "6 months to a year,"
  • the available 2nm/3nm foundry capacity with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) (TSMWF), and
  • the expanding new advanced packaging facility in Singapore, to address the growing backlog.

These robust infrastructure growth prospects also built upon AVGO's excellent, recurring infrastructure software business including VMware Cloud Foundation [VCF] across compute, storage, network, container and cloud management in private/public clouds.

This is with FY2025 revenues of $27.02B (+25.8% YoY) aided by the robust insights offered by the higher bookings at $10.4B in FQ4'25 (+26.8% YoY) and the consequently growing SaaS backlog of $73B (+48.9% YoY).

Combined with the growing pricing power for the infrastructure software segment at 93% in FQ4'25 (+2 points YoY) and the consequently improved operating leverage at margins of 78% (+6 points YoY), it is apparent that Enterprise demand for AVGO's VCF offerings are rather robust.

These underscore their success "in converting our enterprise customers from perpetual vSphere to the full VCF software stack subscription" along with the "completion of the integration of VMware."

These numbers lends credibility to AVGO's promising FY2026 infrastructure software revenue growth guidance at "low double-digit percentage," with these underscoring its excellent ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom through the infrastructure and SaaS layers.

AVGO's Accelerated Growth Cadence Is Cheaply Valued

The Consensus Forward Estimates

Thanks to the outsized FY2025 performance metrics, the growing backlog/AI revenues, and the promising FQ1'26 guidance, it is unsurprising that the consensus have already raised their forward estimates, with AVGO expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +35.7%/+37% through FY2028.

Most importantly, the current FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $13.68 already exceed the prior estimates of $12.14 and my prior estimates of $13.30 discussed in my last article, with it lending credence to my prior conjecture surrounding the conservative consensus estimates, as observed in the consistent upgrades over the past few months.

This builds upon the growth acceleration against AVGO's 5Y historical growth profile at +21.7%/+25.2% and the 10Y growth at +24.9%/+22.5%, respectively, with it exemplifying their renewed growth prospects arising from their ability to capitalize on the multi-year cloud super cycle and the robust hyperscaler demand for custom AI accelerators.

If anything, readers must note that the ongoing AI boom and the rich cash flows/adj EBITDA generation have already contributed to the healthier balance sheet at a net debt level of $48.96B (+15.9% YoY) and a moderating net-debt-to-adj-EBITDA ratio of 1.13x in FQ4'25, compared to a year ago at 1.82x.

AVGO Valuations

These reasons are also why AVGO looks more compelling at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 32.73x, down from the recent peak of 48.99x and the 1Y mean of 36.41x, thanks to the -17.9% correction from the recent 52 week heights and the ongoing sideways trading since early September 2025.

Despite the notable premium against the 5Y mean of 24.83x and the 5Y pre-pandemic mean of 13.37x, I am of the opinion that the stock remains reasonably valued here, especially when compared to its direct AI accelerator peers, including:

  • NVDA at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 38.59x,
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at 53.82x, and
  • Marvell (MRVL) at 29.72x, respectively.

Based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 32.73x and the consensus adj EPS growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +37%, it is apparent that AVGO remains cheap at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP valuations of 0.88x.

This is compared to the sector median of 1.70x, NVDA at 0.80x, AMD at 1.18x, and MRVL at 0.65x (using the same methodology), thanks to AVGO's ongoing stock price consolidation over the past few months, their ability to capitalize on the multi-year cloud super cycle through the growing custom AI opportunities, and the consequently raised consensus forward estimates.

AVGO Remains A Great Buy

AVGO 5Y Stock Price

For now, the bulls have proven AVGO's trading floor at $320s over the past few months of challenging market developments, as the stock also grows into its previously premium valuations through the sideways trading cadence while consolidating nearer to the 50/100 day moving averages.

AVGO Technical Indicators

These reasons are also why AVGO's technical indicators appear more attractive here, as observed in the oversold RSIs and the bottoming trading volumes - all of which pointing to its robust rebound potential upon the future recovery in AI-related sentiments.

Based on the annualized FQ4'25 adj EPS performance at $7.80 (+37.3% YoY) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 32.73x, it is apparent that there is still a little way to go before the stock may grow into my fair value estimates of $255.20.

Otherwise, based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $17.55, it appears that there is an excellent upside potential of +68.7% to my long-term price target of $574.40.

As a result of the improved margin of safety arising from the sideways trading and consequently, the cheaper valuations/oversold technical indicators, and the established Q4'25 support levels at the $320s, I am of the opinion that AVGO remains highly compelling here.

Combined with their excellent ability to monetize the ongoing AI boom across the infrastructure layer through the growing custom AI accelerator orders/backlog and across the SaaS layers through the improved pricing power/operating leverage, aided by the accelerated growth opportunities arising from the multi-year cloud super cycle, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the AVGO stock here.

data.title: All I Want For Christmas Is... Power: Alphabet's AI Energy Strategy
data.description:
Introduction

One of the companies that I didn’t have the chance to break down yet here, on Seeking Alpha, is Alphabet Inc. (GOOG). I am a shareholder of this company, just like most people who invest in the stock market, and especially those who are interested in AI. Google is everywhere - it’s inside indices, ETFs, and retirement accounts, and this is one of the stocks that rallied significantly throughout this year, even though the company is enormous.

This is my first dedicated coverage of Alphabet, but I am not going to spend time explaining what Google does, because most people reading this already know that. Instead, I want to focus on where Google is heading next, what kind of company it is becoming in 2026 and beyond, and why some of the recent decisions it has made are very important for its future outcomes.

The Intersect Deal

One of the most important recent moves from Alphabet was its decision to acquire Intersect, a data center energy provider, for $4.75 billion. It is a relatively small infrastructure acquisition for a company of this size, but it was done to solve one of the biggest bottlenecks for AI that we are expected to see in the next few years, and that is energy.

In 2026 and 2027, power availability is expected to become a main constraint for AI infrastructure, and we could even enter an energy crisis, according to some estimates. Operating large-scale data centers and training AI models requires far more electricity than today’s grids were designed to handle. If you want exposure to AI without directly investing in AI software or chipmakers, by the way, energy is one of the areas that deserves much more attention. Alphabet clearly understands this, and with this Intersect acquisition, Alphabet is being proactive rather than reactive in a few years down the road.

We could see this deal coming before, as Alphabet took a minority stake in the Intersect in 2024, helping to finance its operations. Now, Google is simply completing a move it has been preparing to do. The $4.75 billion is a very low price, especially relative to what Alphabet is getting.

Intersect has around $15 billion of infrastructure either operating or under construction. Alphabet could acquire it for a third of this number because it is taking not just assets, but the responsibility of continuing to invest in and scale that infrastructure, meaning paying off the debt that Intersect had.

Intersect is a great acquisition because it has focused on a co-location model, where clean energy plants are built directly next to data centers. So Google can build a data center and a power plant side by side and power it directly. That avoids grid congestion and lowers the risk of power shortages affecting both data centers and residential customers.

Another reason why the acquisition was a smart move is that projects like this are not simple. They face regulatory problems, require a lot of land, and have a meaningful public pushback (people say it could have powered residential buildings). Intersect’s Oberon project in California is a good example of that complexity, as it was developed under the Bureau of Land Management’s Desert Renewable Energy Conservation Plan, a process that took years of coordination between different agencies and groups.

The Oberon project is the first to achieve operation through the streamlined approach under the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) Desert Renewable Energy Conservation Plan. The landmark plan was the result of six years of collaboration between the federal government, conservation groups, Native American tribes, the renewable energy industry, utilities, and members of the public, and it designated over 10 million acres of conservation and recreation lands in the southern California desert while centering renewable energy development in designated Development Focus Areas.

This can’t be replicated quickly, so by acquiring Intersect and keeping the entire team in place, Alphabet covers the most difficult parts of infrastructure development. With $15 billion of projects already operating or under construction and a targeted 10.8 gigawatts of capacity by late 2028, this gives Google a major head start with resolving energy problem.

In general, when I step back and look at what hyperscalers do, I understand why those companies are ahead everyone else. Their decisions look like a long-term chess game, where everyone is preparing several moves ahead. In my last article about CoreWeave (CRWV), I explained that Nvidia (NVDA) works with them to reduce the risk of hyperscalers fully internalizing chip production. Hyperscalers, in turn, are building their own chips to reduce dependency on Nvidia, which is also an incredibly important move. Google’s TPUs (tensor processing units) are a good example of this strategy. They will most likely never replace Nvidia, simply because Nvidia has been focused on this space for decades, but they are being successful in taking this interdependence down. It reduces risk and improves negotiating power.

At the same time, it’s interesting to observe how these companies are slowly getting into each other’s line of business. Google, which historically was dominant in advertising, is now growing very fast in cloud, AI, data centers, and infrastructure, which was not the strength of this company until now. Amazon (AMZN), which was a dominant player in cloud, is growing its advertising aggressively, effectively stepping into what Google has been doing all along. Everyone is working on chips, robotics, and energy. Tesla (TSLA) builds robots and energy infrastructure. Amazon builds robots and works on Space projects. Google builds robots, chips, cars, and now energy.

This is all not accidental, because when you run a one-million-dollar company, the sky is the limit for you, but when you run an almost 4 billion dollar enterprise, you quickly run out of industries, so you have to add more and more if you want to grow. Alphabet is one of the strongest players, and one of those that will be the most successful in its growth.

Financials

Alphabet is in a very strong financial position to make these kinds of long-term bets. The company currently holds close to $100 billion in cash and has roughly $44 billion in total debt, so it’s not leveraged. It does not need financial engineering to fund acquisitions, or add to capital expenditures, and make constant buybacks, and you want Alphabet to continue, because this management for sure knows what it is doing.

Over the past few years, Alphabet’s return on invested capital (ROIC) has climbed from below 16% in 2020 to more than 32% today. That is an exceptional level and far above its weighted average cost of capital (WACC), meaning the company adds tremendous value from each invested dollar. When a company has a metric like that, it should just continue making assumptions and placing bets, because it is clearly working out amazingly. So, Alphabet’s rising capital expenditures make sense in that context, and I personally don’t view this as a major red flag. It is true that eventually we could face an oversupply of computing power, but with Alphabet's size, this company can put that capacity to use to power and improve its own operations, and to train its own models.

In Q3 2025, revenue grew at a mid-teens pace year over year, operating income increased, and net income jumped meaningfully even after accounting for regulatory fines that pressured margins. Google Cloud continues to stand out, with strong revenue growth and expanding operating margins, which is very important because Cloud is the backbone of Alphabet’s AI ambitions.

Free cash flow remains solid despite very heavy capital expenditures. Net change in cash can move around from year to year, but Alphabet remains comfortably cash-flow positive even while investing aggressively into CapEx, and this is the only real metric I care about at the moment. Don’t get me wrong, net income and cash from operations are as well important, but there you have a lot of rules and ground to “play around” with the numbers. Accounting is very flexible these days. But net change in cash is what this company really holds in their hands after the quarter ends, and as long as it’s in a breakeven or plus after capital expenditures, I am not worried about overspending.

Valuation

Some investors argue that Google looks expensive on traditional valuation metrics. I understand that view, but I also think that sector comparisons do not really capture what Alphabet has become. The communication services sector is too broad a category to do justice to a company that combines advertising, cloud, AI infrastructure, hardware, and now energy. Whether the company is priced fairly relies on traditional metrics that are relative to the sector, but how can you compare the hyperscalers to other companies on the market, when they are those who powered the growth for the rest of the S&P and Nasdaq indices in the last few years?

When I look at Google, I focus more on growth durability and reinvestment quality than on multiples. A company of this size growing at double-digit rates is very rare, and the compounding effect of reinvesting at high returns on capital is more important to me than whether the stock looks optically cheap at any given moment. Of course, if the multiple was at Palantir’s (PLTR) levels, I would say that no matter the premium for the excellence, there is a limit to this stretch. In Alphabet’s case, with a TTM PEG ratio slightly above the sector, Alphabet is fairly valued at the moment.

Conclusion

I would not expect explosive upside from Google from here. The stock has already had a strong run, and we have seen in the past that even Google can experience sharp drawdowns when sentiment shifts or some major correction happens, like in April of 2025.

For me, Google is a long-term compounder. If you are not a shareholder and want exposure, current levels look reasonable for a long-term position. I think these levels will look really attractive a few years down the road. If you already own it, this is the kind of company I am comfortable adding gradually over time, especially on pullbacks. The balance sheet is strong, execution is strong, and the strategic moves being made today, especially around energy and infrastructure, may turn out to be decisive advantages a few years from now.

data.title: POET Technologies: Is It The Next Takeover Target After Marvell's Celestial AI Deal?
data.description:
Investment thesis

POET Technologies (POET) shares have surged more than 65% over the last month due to a number of fundamental factors linked to talk of strategic potential for the company after the acquisition of Celestial AI by custom chip giant Marvell Technology (MRVL). Even though this deal isn't directly related to POET, it has an indirect impact because the company's tech is used in Celestial AI's flagship product, the Photonic Fabric. As a result, the market participants see this deal as a key signal of the importance of POET's photonics technologies in improving data centers and upgrading their functionality for the development of the AI industry. Therefore, my rating for POET has been revised from "Hold" to "Buy," whereas MRVL remains at "Buy." For me, POET stock is still a speculative play. But the potential that's building around the MRVL and Celestial AI deal demonstrates POET's importance in creating a data center value chain. Furthermore, I believe there's a possibility of a company like POET becoming the target of MRVL's next acquisition.

My past assessment of POET

From the time of my last publication on POET, the company's value has increased by +82.49%. I already mentioned in the introduction to this article the main reason for this growth: Possible strategic potential for the company from the completion of Marvell Technology's acquisition of Celestica AI. Sure, you could say this growth is a result of "irrational optimism." But it’s not without reason as market players who already believed in POET’s photonics tech now have a bigger reason to think they were right. Even though I was more skeptical about POET shares before, that was a well-reasoned assessment, primarily based on the results of a fundamental analysis of the company's financial profile and the risks that affect its strategic growth potential. And the main idea behind my "Hold" recommendation for POET shares was the need to wait for the period of time when the company would report its first larger orders from customers, allowing it to generate the necessary cash flow from its operating activities.

What are the details of the deal, and what does it mean for Marvell?

During its presentation of its quarterly results for Q3 2025, Marvell Technology announced the conclusion of a deal to acquire Celestial AI for $3.25 billion. This company is a big deal because of its flagship product, Photonic Fabric, that helps MRVL improve connectivity in next-gen data centers. The expanded tech capabilities of Marvell should be a plus for working with customers (data centers) building their infrastructure using custom chips. And it's Celestial AI's technology that makes the overall functionality of data centers using MRVL products more energy efficient and faster in data transfer. In my opinion, this is the main competitive advantage that reflects the quality of solutions offered by companies working on the development of digital infrastructure for the AI industry.

The importance of this deal for Marvell and the future of POET can be understood better by looking at how Photonic Fabric works. The foundation of Photonic Fabric is optical connections that make data transfer faster and more reliable. It's a next-gen tech that totally gets rid of the tech issues with traditional electrical signals. With increased data transfer speed and reliability, there's also an improvement in the energy efficiency of computing processes in data centers. Together with the optical connection, Marvell receives specialized processors (XPUs) from Celestial AI, developed to solve problems related to improving the performance of AI applications.

Given that Marvell is one of the two major players in the global custom chip market (alongside Broadcom), acquiring XPU enables it to create a more competitive offering for data center customers. The custom MRVL chips are less expensive and position the company among the leading suppliers in the AI industry. Since power consumption is one of the key barriers to the development of the new digital economy, energy efficiency technologies are key tools for product improvement. This is why the acquisition of Celestial AI is so important for MRVL, as it essentially offers a "gold mine" for future cash flow.

The importance of POET technology for Marvell's development

Given that Marvell is acquiring Celestial AI in part to obtain next-generation optical connectivity, it's also interested in the technologies that underpin the development of this product. Offering optical interconnect solutions, POET creates photonic solutions at the chip level. It does so by integrating photonics and electronics for vertical market applications. These innovations enable stable operation of networks used in AI technologies. This is essentially the first technology that allows the integration of photonics and electronics into a single device. With the rapid development of data centers, this capability will offer the market a unique solution that will increase stability and data transfer speeds. And most importantly, make it cheaper by reducing energy consumption.

The advantage of the POET optical interposer is the ability to build optical data transmission mechanisms based on light in such a way as to make it as economical and efficient as possible, minimizing manufacturing and energy costs. All of the product is formed on a single chip, allowing for the minimization of space in data center equipment. These advantages of POET technology make it possible to scale data centers to large volumes, increasing the economic efficiency of photonic solutions many times over. For more details on the features of POET technology, I discussed them in my previous article.

How does POET benefit from this?

The successful integration of Marvell with the Celestial AI platform will lead to the successful application of optical interconnect technologies. It will significantly increase bandwidth and data transfer rates, both of which play a key role in AI computing. Analyzing the relationship between Celestial AI and POET, we should note that the two companies have formed a strong technological partnership. Within the framework of the development of the Photonic Fabric platform, the POET technology is the basis for Celestial AI's developments in the field of integrating photonics into custom chips. Thanks to this, it's possible to create the high-speed solutions necessary for the operation of AI applications and data centers.

Basically, Celestial AI and POET are in the same business, just in different areas. So, POET develops tech that makes it possible to speed up data transfer using photonics, but Celestial AI already uses this tech in their systems, directly integrated into working data centers. Beginning in 2022, POET is mentioned in Celestial AI's reports, albeit as a customer to whom the technology was supplied. Based on Celestial AI's innovations, a joint development project was carried out to create a platform of optical interposers, allowing the company to offer the market a cutting-edge solution for high-speed computing.

Now that Marvell has announced its intention to acquire Celestial AI, MRVL will gain access to POET-dependent technologies through this deal. With Marvell becoming an IT giant, finding the best solutions for developing its specialized microchips, which are widely used in data centers, has become a priority. Since POET offers a specific solution that Celestial AI has implemented, this creates conditions that allow POET itself to offer its technologies to solve MRVL's problems.

Why is Marvell the future of the AI and data center industry?

In order to understand Marvell's competitive advantage in today's AI and data center industry, we need to look at the differences between its business model and that of the industry leader, Nvidia. There's a fundamental difference in their approaches to chip design. Whereas Nvidia creates universal chips with multitasking and multifunctional capabilities, Marvell's custom chips are highly specialized and designed to perform routine, repeatable tasks. AI's technological boom is impacting the semiconductor and microchip industry due to the need to meet demand from new data centers. Every day, large-scale routine tasks are performed within their structure. In order to ensure that these tasks are completed, custom chips are used, since they're cheaper and easier to manufacture.

Because of this, many cloud tech giants, such as Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Amazon (AMZN), have turned to custom chip manufacturers to tailor chips to their own algorithms built into their software.
